producer.title: Producer
producer.text: The domaine (estate) that grew the grapes and made the wine. "Mis en bouteille au domaine" means estate-bottled, a quality signal in Burgundy.

appellation.title: Appellation — The Hidden Grape
appellation.text: Chablis is ALWAYS 100% Chardonnay. No grape is listed because the appellation legally requires it. This is the core Old World principle: the place name carries the grape information.

classification.title: Quality Classification
classification.text: Premier Cru is the second-highest vineyard tier in Burgundy (below Grand Cru). It signals superior terroir, stricter yields, and higher minimum alcohol.

vineyard.title: Named Vineyard (Climat)
vineyard.text: "Fourchaume" is a specific Premier Cru vineyard on the Right Bank of the Serein river in Chablis. In Burgundy, the vineyard name tells you more about character than almost anything else.

vintage.title: Vintage Year
vintage.text: The year the grapes were harvested. 2020 was warm in Chablis, producing richer, rounder wines than the steelier 2019. Vintage is your weather report in a number.

legal.title: Legal Appellation Statement
legal.text: "Appellation Controlee" (now AOP) is France's guarantee that the wine meets all legal requirements for that named region: grape varieties, yields, winemaking methods, geography.

technical.title: Volume & ABV
technical.text: 750ml standard bottle. 13% ABV, moderate-to-full for white Burgundy. Higher ABV often indicates warmer vintages and riper fruit.

origin.title: Country of Origin
origin.text: Legally required for export. Confirms French AOC/AOP regulations govern this wine's production.

lines.text: Denominazione di Origine Controllata e Garantita

producer.title: Producer (Castello)
producer.text: An estate in the heart of the Chianti Classico zone. "Castello" (castle) names are common in Tuscany, many wineries occupy historic fortified properties.

appellation.title: Appellation — Place as Grape
appellation.text: Chianti Classico must be at least 80% Sangiovese by DOCG law. No grape is listed. "Classico" means the original, historic zone, distinct from the much larger basic "Chianti" area.

classification.title: Riserva — Legal Aging
classification.text: In Italy, Riserva is legally defined: Chianti Classico Riserva requires minimum 24 months aging, including 3 months in bottle. This is NOT a marketing term, it carries legal force.

legal.title: DOCG Classification
legal.text: DOCG (Denominazione di Origine Controllata e Garantita) is Italy's highest tier. The wine passed chemical analysis AND a tasting panel. Italy's pyramid: Vino, IGT, DOC, DOCG.

vintage.title: Vintage Year
vintage.text: 2019 is widely considered exceptional in Tuscany: elegant, layered, with long aging potential.

technical.title: Volume & ABV
technical.text: 13.5%, typical for medium-to-full Sangiovese. Tuscan Sangiovese tends toward higher acidity and moderate alcohol.

origin.title: Country of Origin
origin.text: Confirms Italian DOCG regulations apply, governing grape varieties, yields, aging, and geographic boundaries.

producer.title: Producer (Bodega)
producer.text: Lopez de Heredia, founded 1877. One of Rioja's oldest and most traditional producers. Known for decades-long aging before release.

brand.title: Wine/Brand Name
brand.text: "Vina Tondonia" is the estate's flagship wine, named after its vineyard. In Spain, wines often carry proprietary brand names alongside the regional appellation.

appellation.title: Regional Appellation
appellation.text: Rioja reds are traditionally Tempranillo-dominant, often blended with Garnacha, Graciano, or Mazuelo. No grape listed, Old World convention.

classification.title: Reserva — Aging Hierarchy
classification.text: Spain's legally defined aging tiers for red Rioja: Joven, Crianza (1yr oak, 2yr total), Reserva (1yr oak, 3yr total), Gran Reserva (2yr oak, 5yr total).

legal.title: DOCa — Highest Spanish Tier
legal.text: DOCa (Denominacion de Origen Calificada) is Spain's top classification. Only Rioja and Priorat hold this status.

vintage.title: Vintage Year
vintage.text: 2011 on a Reserva released many years later. Traditional Rioja producers age extensively before release.

technical.title: Volume & ABV
technical.text: 13.5%, typical for aged Rioja. Extended barrel and bottle aging can integrate alcohol and soften tannins.

origin.title: Country of Origin
origin.text: Confirms Spanish DOCa regulations. Aging terms have different legal requirements than Italian or French ones.

producer.title: Weingut (Estate)
producer.text: "Weingut" means wine estate. Joh. Jos. Prum is one of the Mosel's legendary Riesling producers.

vineyard.title: Village + Vineyard
vineyard.text: "Wehlener Sonnenuhr" = the Sonnenuhr vineyard in the village of Wehlen. German labels use Village+Vineyard format. Sonnenuhr ("sundial") is one of the most famous steep-slope Mosel sites.

classification.title: Pradikat Level — Ripeness Scale
classification.text: Spatlese ("late harvest") is a ripeness level: Kabinett, Spatlese, Auslese, Beerenauslese, Trockenbeerenauslese, Eiswein. Each requires higher grape sugar.

legal.title: Qualitatswein mit Pradikat
legal.text: Germany's highest quality category (now Pradikatswein). No chaptalization allowed. Below: Qualitatswein, Landwein, Deutscher Wein.

appellation.title: Wine Region
appellation.text: Mosel: steep slate slopes along the river create intense, mineral-driven Rieslings with piercing acidity and often low alcohol.

vintage.title: Vintage Year
vintage.text: Vintage is especially important in Germany's cool climate. The difference between years dramatically affects ripeness and style.

technical.title: Volume & ABV
technical.text: 7.5%, remarkably low. Classic Mosel Riesling: residual sugar balanced by razor acidity. Low alcohol preserves delicacy.

origin.title: Estate Bottled & Origin
origin.text: "Erzeugerabfullung" means estate-bottled. The producer grew the grapes and made the wine.

lines.text: Produced & Bottled by Caymus Vineyards, Rutherford, CA

producer.title: Brand/Producer
producer.text: In the New World, the brand is often the biggest text. The name carries the quality signal, not a classification system.

grape.title: Grape Variety — Front and Center
grape.text: "Cabernet Sauvignon": the New World convention. The GRAPE is the star. This is the opposite of Old World labels.

appellation.title: AVA (American Viticultural Area)
appellation.text: "Napa Valley" is an AVA, a legally defined area. Less prescriptive than European appellations. 85% of grapes must come from the named AVA.

vintage.title: Vintage Year
vintage.text: 2019 is a great Napa vintage. US law requires 95% of grapes from the stated vintage year.

technical.title: Volume & ABV
technical.text: 14.8%, high alcohol typical of warm Napa Cabernet. Reflects full ripeness and rich, full-bodied style.

origin.title: Bottling Statement
origin.text: "Produced & Bottled by" means the winery fermented and bottled at least 75%. Different from "Cellared and Bottled" which indicates less involvement.

producer.title: Brand
producer.text: Cloudy Bay put Marlborough Sauvignon Blanc on the world map in the 1980s. New World labels emphasize the brand.

grape.title: Grape Variety
grape.text: Sauvignon Blanc, front and center, New World style. In New Zealand, the grape must be at least 85% of the wine.

appellation.title: Region
appellation.text: Marlborough: internationally famous for intensely aromatic Sauvignon Blanc with passion fruit, cut grass, and crisp acidity.

vintage.title: Vintage Year
vintage.text: 2023. Most Marlborough Sauvignon Blanc is made for immediate drinking. Freshness is the point.

technical.title: Volume & ABV
technical.text: 13%, moderate. Cool maritime climate preserves high acidity while achieving good ripeness.

origin.title: Country of Origin
origin.text: New Zealand's GI system is less prescriptive than European appellations. More winemaker freedom.

lines.text: Elabore par Moet & Chandon, Epernay, France

producer.title: House (Maison)
producer.text: Moet & Chandon is the parent house. In Champagne: NM (Negociant-Manipulant) buys grapes; RM (Recoltant-Manipulant) is a grower-producer.

brand.title: Prestige Cuvee
brand.text: Dom Perignon is a "prestige cuvee": always vintage, only produced in exceptional years. The house decides whether to "declare" a vintage.

sweetness.title: Dosage / Sweetness Level
sweetness.text: "Brut" = dry (<12g/L sugar). The scale: Brut Nature, Extra Brut, Brut, Extra Dry, Sec, Demi-Sec, Doux. "Extra Dry" is confusingly sweeter than "Brut."

vintage.title: Vintage Declaration
vintage.text: A vintage year means ALL grapes are from that year. Most Champagne is NV (blending years for consistency). Vintage = exceptional year statement.

legal.title: AOC Champagne
legal.text: Only sparkling wine from the Champagne region using methode champenoise can be called "Champagne." Grapes and methods are strictly regulated.

technical.title: Volume & ABV
technical.text: 12.5%, typical. Cool climate + second fermentation in bottle. The base wine is high-acid, moderate alcohol.

origin.title: Elabore par (Made by)
origin.text: Look for fine print codes: NM = buys grapes, RM = grower-producer, CM = cooperative. This tells you the production model.

lines.text: Denominazione di Origine Controllata e Garantita

producer.title: Producer
producer.text: Giacomo Conterno: legendary Barolo producer known for traditional long-maceration winemaking. Producer reputation is paramount in Barolo.

appellation.title: Appellation — Nebbiolo's Kingdom
appellation.text: Barolo is always 100% Nebbiolo. No grape listed, DOCG defines it. Must age 38 months minimum (18 in wood). Riserva requires 62 months.

vineyard.title: MGA (Vineyard Site)
vineyard.text: "Francia" is a specific MGA (Menzione Geografica Aggiuntiva) in Serralunga d'Alba. Since 2010, Barolo has 181 officially designated vineyard sites, like Burgundy's climat system.

legal.title: DOCG
legal.text: Barolo DOCG: 100% Nebbiolo, minimum 38 months aging, minimum 13% ABV. These are legal requirements, not suggestions.

vintage.title: Vintage Year
vintage.text: 2019 is part of the celebrated 2019-2020-2021 Barolo triumvirate. Powerful and structured, rewards patience.

technical.title: Volume & ABV
technical.text: 14.5%, high but typical. Nebbiolo achieves high sugar during its long, late ripening (often harvested October). Tannin and acidity balance the alcohol.

origin.title: Village of Origin
origin.text: "Serralunga d'Alba": one of five key communes. Serralunga = most structured, powerful Barolos. La Morra = more perfumed, elegant.

producer.title: Chateau
producer.text: "Chateau" = Bordeaux wine estate. Leoville-Las Cases is a Deuxieme Grand Cru Classe (Second Growth), often called a "Super Second" rivaling First Growths.

brand.title: Grand Vin
brand.text: "Grand Vin" = the chateau's primary wine, their best production. Most serious chateaux also make a "Second Wine" from younger vines.

brand2.title: Historic Name
brand2.text: The full historical name connecting the wine to its aristocratic lineage. These names have been on labels for centuries.

appellation.title: Commune Appellation
appellation.text: Saint-Julien: one of six most prestigious Medoc communes. Sub-appellation within Haut-Medoc within Bordeaux. More specificity = higher quality.

vintage.title: Vintage Year
vintage.text: 2016 is one of the great recent Bordeaux vintages: structured, classic, built for decades of aging.

classification.title: 1855 Classification
classification.text: The historic ranking of estates into five tiers (First to Fifth Growth). Barely changed in 170 years, frozen in time.

legal.title: AOC Statement
legal.text: Legally defines origin, permitted grapes (Cabernet Sauvignon, Merlot, Cabernet Franc, Petit Verdot), and production methods.

technical.title: Volume & ABV
technical.text: 13.5%, moderate for Left Bank Bordeaux. Maritime climate keeps alcohol in check.

origin.title: Chateau Bottled
origin.text: "Mis en bouteille au Chateau" = estate-bottled. The gold standard in Bordeaux, guaranteeing provenance.

producer.title: Brand/Producer
producer.text: Catena Zapata: Argentina's most celebrated producer, credited with proving Malbec's potential as world-class.

grape.title: Grape Variety
grape.text: Malbec: Argentina's signature grape, originally from Cahors, France. In Mendoza's high altitude it produces deeply colored wines with violet and plum character.

vineyard.title: Single Vineyard
vineyard.text: "Adrianna Vineyard" at over 1,500m altitude. Naming the vineyard signals a premium, terroir-focused wine. New World embracing Old World specificity.

appellation.title: Sub-Region & District
appellation.text: "Gualtallary, Tupungato": a specific district within the Uco Valley. Higher altitude = cooler nights, better acidity, more complexity.

region.title: Broader Region
region.text: Mendoza produces ~70% of Argentina's wine. A desert irrigated by Andean snowmelt: extreme altitude, intense UV, dramatic day-night temperature swings.

vintage.title: Vintage Year
vintage.text: 2019. At high altitude, vintage variation matters. Extreme UV and temperature swings give distinctive intensity.

technical.title: Volume & ABV
technical.text: 14.0%, typical for high-altitude Malbec. Strong sun concentrates sugars but cool nights preserve acidity.

origin.title: Estate Grown & Bottled
origin.text: The producer controlled everything from vine to bottle, signaling commitment to site-specific winemaking.

quizQuestions.question: This label says 'Chablis' but no grape variety is listed. What grape is this wine made from?
quizQuestions.explanation: Chablis is always 100% Chardonnay. The appellation legally requires it. Old World labels name the place; the grape is implied.

quizQuestions.question: Why is 'Cabernet Sauvignon' the most prominent text after the brand name?
options: New World labels lead with grape variety
It's legally required to be largest
quizQuestions.explanation: New World convention: the grape variety is the primary identifier. Old World labels show the place name instead.

quizQuestions.question: What is the primary grape in Chianti Classico?
quizQuestions.explanation: Chianti Classico must be at least 80% Sangiovese by DOCG law. The place name tells you the grape.

quizQuestions.question: What does 'Marlborough' tell you about this wine's style?
options: It will be oaky and rich
It will be aromatic with high acidity
quizQuestions.explanation: Marlborough's cool maritime climate produces intensely aromatic Sauvignon Blanc with passion fruit, cut grass, and crisp acidity.

quizQuestions.question: What is the dominant grape in red Rioja?
quizQuestions.explanation: Red Rioja is traditionally Tempranillo-dominant, often blended with Garnacha, Graciano, or Mazuelo.

quizQuestions.question: This wine is 14.8% ABV. What does that suggest?
quizQuestions.explanation: High ABV (14%+) indicates warm climate and fully ripe grapes. Napa's valleys produce rich, high-alcohol Cabernets.

quizQuestions.question: This Mosel Riesling is only 7.5% ABV. Why so low?
options: Residual sugar remains instead of converting to alcohol
quizQuestions.explanation: Fermentation is stopped before all sugar converts to alcohol. The result: low ABV, residual sweetness balanced by high acidity.

quizQuestions.question: What grape is Barolo made from?
quizQuestions.explanation: Barolo is always 100% Nebbiolo. Another Old World label where the place tells you the grape.

quizQuestions.question: What does 'Brut' mean on a Champagne label?
quizQuestions.explanation: Brut means dry. The sweetness scale: Brut Nature, Extra Brut, Brut, Extra Dry, Sec, Demi-Sec, Doux.

quizQuestions.question: Most Champagne is 'NV' (Non-Vintage). What does a vintage year mean?
options: All grapes are from a single exceptional year
quizQuestions.explanation: Vintage Champagne means all grapes from that year, which the house considered exceptional enough to declare.

quizQuestions.question: This Argentine wine leads with 'Malbec.' Where did Malbec originally come from?
quizQuestions.explanation: Malbec originated in Cahors, southwest France. It was brought to Argentina in the mid-1800s and found its greatest expression in Mendoza.

quizQuestions.question: What is the primary red grape of Bordeaux's Left Bank (Medoc)?
quizQuestions.explanation: Cabernet Sauvignon dominates the Left Bank. Merlot dominates the Right Bank. Knowing which bank tells you the blend.

quizQuestions.question: What does 'Riserva' legally guarantee on this Italian label?
options: Minimum 24 months aging including 3 months in bottle
quizQuestions.explanation: Riserva is legally defined: Chianti Classico Riserva requires minimum 24 months total aging, at least 3 months in bottle.

quizQuestions.question: Put Spain's aging terms in order from youngest to oldest:
quizQuestions.explanation: Joven, Crianza (1yr oak), Reserva (1yr oak, 3yr total), Gran Reserva (2yr oak, 5yr total).

quizQuestions.question: What does DOCG mean and how does it differ from DOC?
options: DOCG adds a guarantee: wines are tasted and chemically tested
DOCG means the wine is older
quizQuestions.explanation: DOCG adds "Garantita": wines must pass chemical analysis AND a tasting panel. Italy's pyramid: Vino, IGT, DOC, DOCG.

quizQuestions.question: What does 'Spatlese' indicate on a German label?
options: A specific ripeness level: late harvest
The wine was aged in oak
quizQuestions.explanation: Spatlese ("late harvest") is a Pradikat ripeness level. It does NOT necessarily mean sweet. Spatlese can be dry (trocken) or sweet.

quizQuestions.question: What is DOCa and which Spanish regions hold this classification?
options: Spain's entry level, all regions have it
Spain's highest, only Rioja and Priorat
quizQuestions.explanation: DOCa is Spain's top classification. Only Rioja and Priorat have earned this status.

quizQuestions.question: How much of the wine must come from the named AVA?
quizQuestions.explanation: US law requires 85% of grapes from the stated AVA. If a specific vineyard is named, 95% must come from it.

quizQuestions.question: What is the hierarchy of Burgundy vineyard classifications?
options: Grand Cru, Premier Cru, Village, Regional
Regional, Village, Premier Cru, Grand Cru
Premier Cru, Grand Cru, Village, Regional
quizQuestions.explanation: From top: Grand Cru (1-2% of production), Premier Cru, Village, Regional (Bourgogne). Each reflects superior terroir.

quizQuestions.question: What does 'Grand Cru Classe en 1855' refer to?
options: The historic 1855 Classification ranking estates into five tiers
quizQuestions.explanation: The 1855 Classification ranked estates into First through Fifth Growths. It has barely changed in 170 years.

quizQuestions.question: Which is SWEETER: 'Brut' or 'Extra Dry' Champagne?
quizQuestions.explanation: Counter-intuitively, Extra Dry (12-17g/L) is sweeter than Brut (0-12g/L). This trips up almost everyone on WSET exams.

quizQuestions.question: What is the minimum aging requirement for Barolo DOCG?
quizQuestions.explanation: Barolo requires 38 months total, at least 18 in wood. Riserva requires 62 months.

quizQuestions.question: Why is 'Gualtallary, Tupungato' more specific than just 'Mendoza'?
options: It identifies a specific high-altitude district
quizQuestions.explanation: Gualtallary is a district in the Uco Valley. Argentina increasingly uses sub-regional specificity: higher altitude = cooler nights, more complexity.

quizQuestions.question: What does 'Mis en bouteille au Chateau' mean?
quizQuestions.explanation: Estate-bottled: made and bottled at the chateau. The gold standard in Bordeaux, guaranteeing provenance.

quizQuestions.question: "Wehlener Sonnenuhr" — what does this naming format tell you?
quizQuestions.explanation: German Village+Vineyard format: "Wehlener" (from Wehlen) + "Sonnenuhr" (sundial vineyard). The "-er" suffix converts the village name to an adjective.

quizQuestions.question: What does 'Qualitatswein mit Pradikat' prohibit that lower tiers allow?
quizQuestions.explanation: Pradikatswein prohibits chaptalization. All sugar must come from the grape. This is why Pradikat levels are tied to natural ripeness.

quizQuestions.question: Why would knowing the specific climat (vineyard) matter in Burgundy?
options: Burgundy classifies quality by specific vineyard site
quizQuestions.explanation: Burgundy's system is built on terroir. Specific sites produce distinct wines. Two plots 50 meters apart can be different quality tiers.

quizQuestions.question: 'Produced & Bottled by' vs 'Cellared & Bottled by' — what's the difference?
quizQuestions.explanation: "Produced & Bottled" = fermented and bottled 75%+. "Cellared & Bottled" = may have only stored and bottled purchased wine.

quizQuestions.question: What's the difference between 'Chianti' and 'Chianti Classico'?
options: Classico is the original, smaller, historic zone with stricter rules
quizQuestions.explanation: Chianti Classico is the original medieval zone between Florence and Siena: much smaller and higher-quality than the expanded "Chianti" DOCG.

quizQuestions.question: Order the German Pradikat levels from least to most ripe:
quizQuestions.explanation: Kabinett, Spatlese, Auslese, Beerenauslese (BA), Trockenbeerenauslese (TBA). Each requires higher must weight at harvest.

quizQuestions.question: On a Champagne label, what's the difference between NM and RM?
options: NM buys grapes (Negociant), RM grows their own (Recoltant)
quizQuestions.explanation: NM buys grapes, most big houses. RM grows their own, the 'grower Champagne' movement. Both can be excellent.

quizQuestions.question: What does 'MGA' mean on a Barolo label?
quizQuestions.explanation: MGA (Menzione Geografica Aggiuntiva) is Barolo's vineyard designation system with 181 named sites: Italy's answer to Burgundy's climats.

quizQuestions.question: Which Barolo commune is known for the most structured, powerful wines?
quizQuestions.explanation: Serralunga d'Alba = most structured, powerful, long-lived. La Morra = more perfumed and elegant. The commune shapes the style.

quizQuestions.question: What does 'Grand Vin' on a Bordeaux label indicate?
quizQuestions.explanation: "Grand Vin" = the chateau's top selection, distinct from their "Second Wine" made from younger vines or lesser lots.

quizQuestions.question: Why would an Argentine label name a specific vineyard like 'Adrianna Vineyard'?
options: To signal a premium, terroir-focused wine
quizQuestions.explanation: Single-vineyard on a New World label = the producer believes that site produces distinctive wine. New World adopting Old World's terroir philosophy.

quizQuestions.question: Bordeaux has nested appellations: Bordeaux, Haut-Medoc, Saint-Julien. What does this nesting indicate?
options: Greater specificity = higher quality and stricter rules
quizQuestions.explanation: More geographic specificity signals higher quality. 'Saint-Julien' meets stricter standards than just 'Bordeaux.' The more specific, the more it tells you.
